notes.note: M. Claudius Marcellus, consul 51 BC, a leading optimate pardoned by Caesar; murdered at Athens (or Piraeus) in May 45 BC on his way home. Cassius and then Servius Sulpicius sent Cicero the news, which shocked Rome.
Caerellia, a wealthy older woman and devotee of Cicero's philosophy, had somehow obtained advance copies of his works; Cicero is annoyed but acquits Atticus's copyists, suspecting another channel.
Cicero worries about which version of his text reaches whom: an outdated draft must not go to Brutus, nor an unfinished one to Balbus, Caesar's powerful agent who expected a finished copy.

notes.anchor: you are dunning the assigned debtors
notes.note: Attributi were debtors formally assigned (by attributio) to a creditor in settlement of an obligation; Atticus is collecting on such assignments on Cicero's behalf.

M. Tullius, Cicero's clerk (scriba), who had kept his provincial accounts; distinct from Cicero himself and from the freedman Tiro. A sum of Cicero's was in his hands, earmarked for the gardens purchase.

notes.anchor: under the head of a vow
notes.note: Money set aside nomine voti, dedicated to fulfil a religious vow; Cicero clarifies that the sum held by his secretary was not so consecrated but is his own, to be spent on the project.

Joint heirs (coheredes) to one of the estates Cicero had entered; co-heirship required collective action, so settling matters without Atticus present would be onerous. The Latin clause here is textually corrupt.
